FT_IC1.evidence: Methods section states: "Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study, we assessed the association between cyberbullying victimization and substance use initiation among adolescents."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with cross-sectional and longitudinal logistic regression examining cyberbullying victimization and substance initiation in ABCD Study participants (N=10,335 at year 2; N=6095 at year 3).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Drug and Alcohol Dependence, 2023. No evidence of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper analyzes cyberbullying victimization (IV) and substance initiation (DV). Per BINDING clarification §2.4: "Cyberbullying alone does not qualify." Paper contains no separately-measured phone/SM variable paired with cyberbullying. Cyberbullying is the sole exposure variable; no Step 1 phone/SM-specific estimate found, and Step 2 composite analysis not applicable.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Paper does not qualify as containing a phone/SM variable per IC2 binding rules.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "Data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study of 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from September 2016 to January 2021 were used..."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis with cross-sectional and longitudinal linear mixed models examining adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines (physical activity, screen time, sleep) and outcomes (cognition, psychosocial health, zBMI, gray matter volume).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Journal of Adolescent Health, 2023. No evidence of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: No isolated phone/SM-specific estimate found. The headline composite is 24-hour movement guidelines combining sleep, physical activity, and screen time with single coefficients per category, not separate per-modality breakouts. Step 2: Composite 24-MB includes sleep (9-11 hours), physical activity (≥60 min), and screen time (≤2 hours). Screen time qualifies as phone/SM, but it is bundled with non-phone/non-SM items (sleep duration, physical activity) without separate breakout for screen time alone. Per criteria: composite with TV/non-SM items bundled without breakout fails Step 2. NOT_MET.

notes: Exclusion code: FT-EC2. Composite includes non-screen-time items (sleep, activity) without isolated screen-time coefficient. No phone/SM-specific variable qualifies per IC2 Step 1 or Step 2.

FT_IC1.evidence: Methods: "We analyzed data from the ABCD Study, a demographically diverse study of development across adolescence...including 11,875 children aged 9-10 from 21 sites in the United States."

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using mixed-effect models examining associations between self-reported sleep and screen time (social media, video gaming) in ABCD participants across pre-pandemic and pandemic timepoints.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed publication in Health Psychology, 2023. No evidence of duplication.

FT_IC2.evidence: Step 1: Paper isolates phone/SM-specific variables in results. Methods state "six screen activities (internet browsing, texting, video chatting, social media use, playing single player, and playing multiplayer video games)." Results report "time spent on social media platforms" and "time spent on single player video games" as separate variables analyzed. Sleep (time in bed, bedtime, sleep onset latency) is individual-trait-level outcome. Main effects reported: "time spent on social media platforms (χ²(1) = 114.56, p<.01)" and "time spent on single player video games (χ²(1) = 6.13, p = .013)" significantly associated with time in bed. Phone/SM-specific variables paired with individual-trait sleep outcome. MET.

FT_IC3.evidence: Quantitative results reported throughout. Examples: regression coefficients (slopes with p-values), χ² tests, confidence intervals for sleep measures (time in bed, bedtime, sleep onset latency). Results table S2-S7 provide multiple quantitative estimates for SM↔sleep relationships.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with numeric results present in main text and supplements. Mixed-effect models with unstandardized estimates and p-values reported. Example: social media × assessment interaction (χ²_social media × assessment(6) = 117.19, p < .01).

FT_EC7_reason: Paper contains longitudinal design; however, screen time (SM/social media) appears as IV in analyses predicting sleep outcomes. Additionally, analyses include cross-sectional comparisons (e.g., pre-pandemic vs. pandemic assessments treated as separate cross-sectional snapshots). SM is not DV-only: the paper examines screen time as predictor of sleep changes. Condition (b) "SM is DV in every analysis" is not satisfied. FT-EC7 does not apply.
notes: Paper qualifies for inclusion. IC2 MET via Step 1 (isolated social media and video game time as IVs paired with sleep outcomes). IC3-IC4 MET (quantitative results provided). FT-EC7 does not apply (SM serves as IV, not DV-only).
